agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, but the constraints are mutually exclusive when combined.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, which progresses through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing further fulfillment. Intended to be available before shipment per PM requirements."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated for an order that locks in pricing. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating/confirming an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The act of dispatching goods to the customer. PM defines this as the cancellation cutoff point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could narrow or widen this window.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, when a cancellation is requested, then the order is cancelled."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as a hard financial constraint — reversing an invoice has legal, accounting, and audit implications. If violated, financial records become inconsistent.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, when a cancellation is requested, then the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. It could be changed to invoice at shipment or at a different lifecycle stage.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has just been placed, then an invoice exists for that order immediately."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — BR-1 says cancel is allowed (not yet shipped), but BR-2 + BR-3 say it is impossible (already invoiced at placement). This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but shipment is delayed for days","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — The entire window between placement and shipment is simultaneously cancellable (BR-1) and non-cancellable (BR-2 + BR-3)."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Requirements do not address whether partial shipments affect cancellation eligibility."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High. If we implement BR-1 and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance's accounting integrity is violated. If we enforce BR-2 strictly, customers can never cancel, leading to chargebacks, refund requests, and customer churn.","operational":"High. Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing, which directly blocks the PM's cancellation window. Resolving this requires changing either the invoicing timing or the cancellation policy.","user":"High. Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. A system that invoices at placement and forbids cancellation of invoiced orders effectively offers zero cancellation capability, which is hostile to customer experience.","risk":"Critical. Implementing any interpretation without stakeholder alignment will violate at least one team's constraint. Shipping the wrong interpretation could cause financial audit failures or severe customer dissatisfaction.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all if Finance + Ops rules are enforced as stated, leading to frustration and potential churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation is allowed post-invoice, their accounting invariant is broken — revenue recognition, tax implications, and audit trails are compromised."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is deferred to shipment to enable cancellation, they lose the ability to lock in pricing at placement."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints regardless of which interpretation is chosen."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Their stated cancellation policy is unimplementable under current Finance and Ops constraints."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The three requirements contain a direct logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment. BR-3 ensures every placed order is invoiced. BR-2 forbids cancellation of invoiced orders. Therefore: every placed order is invoiced (BR-3), no invoiced order can be cancelled (BR-2), so no placed order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1. No acceptance criteria can be defined until stakeholders resolve this.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — it is a logical impossibility, not an edge case","Invoice irreversibility is likely a true financial invariant","The three rules, taken together, make cancellation impossible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which rule should yield — this is a business decision, not a technical one","Whether 'invoiced' in Finance's constraint means the same thing as the invoice Operations creates at placement","Whether a credit note or void mechanism exists that Finance would accept as distinct from 'reversing' an invoice"],"assumptions":["All three statements reflect current, accurate policy from each team","'Invoice' means the same thing across Finance and Operations contexts","There is no existing credit note or invoice void process that would allow cancellation without technically 'reversing' the invoice"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements are mutually exclusive. PM says cancel before shipment. Ops says invoice at placement. Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. Combining these: every order is invoiced immediately, and no invoiced order can be cancelled, therefore no order can ever be cancelled — which directly contradicts the PM requirement. This is not an edge case; it is a fundamental incompatibility in the stated rules.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["(A) Defer invoicing until shipment — enables PM's cancellation window but Operations loses price-lock at placement. May require a separate 'price lock' or 'order confirmation' concept distinct from invoicing.","(B) Introduce a 'void invoice' or 'credit note' mechanism — Finance treats invoicing as irreversible, but a credit note is a new forward-looking financial event, not a reversal. Cancellation would generate a credit note rather than reversing the invoice. Requires Finance sign-off.","(C) Narrow the cancellation window — PM accepts that cancellation is not possible post-placement, or only possible within a very short grace period before invoicing occurs (e.g., async invoicing with a delay).","(D) Distinguish 'pro forma invoice' from 'tax invoice' — Operations could issue a pro forma (non-binding) at placement for price-lock, with the tax invoice issued at shipment. Only the tax invoice triggers Finance's irreversibility constraint."],"recommendation":"Option D or B. Option D cleanly separates the pricing concern (pro forma) from the financial commitment (tax invoice), preserving all three teams' intent. Option B is the industry-standard approach (credit notes) if true invoice separation is not feasible. Both require a meeting between PM, Finance, and Operations to align on terminology and process before any implementation begins."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are logically contradictory and cannot be implemented as-is.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is impossible to satisfy when BR-2 and BR-3 are both enforced. The conjunction of all three rules produces a system where cancellation is both permitted (BR-1) and forbidden (BR-2 + BR-3) for every order between placement and shipment."}]}
